characterDescription: Black hair and mustache, with flinty grey hairs at the temples, bristly and kinked, as if he'd looked overlong into a smokepit. His eyes and eyebrows are shadowed with the same sooty darkness, bagged and deeply lined, and with irises of such a stony blue that they are almost indiscernible from the pupils. His lips are chapped and pressed together; when they open, the voice that issues from them has a clear and precise depth to it that is naturally discomforting. His expression is more senatorial than grim, a kind of stoical look that is yet untroubled, his features calm. He stands above average height, with ropey and knotted limbs, all sinew and tendons visible beneath sun-darkened skin, and with a slight asymmetry to his stance as his pointed right shoulderblade rises higher than his left. His veined hands are smooth at the fingertips and stained the color of rusty iron. When he stands still, he is upright, solitary, patient. And when he walks, it with ungainly clamber and shuffle of a cormorant. Upon his person he carries a bronze flanged mace with a long and hazelwood haft, and an ash wood targe. He wears a 'shaman coat' - a thick, brown, twill wool lining layered over with deer leather of dappled sepia and pewter and flax-white color. a pair of iron deer hooves hang as ornaments near the waist, beside an oxbone flute, and one set of brass chimes is draped along a shoulder. Moreover, the coat sports three dolls with bodies of fur, and heads of deer hair. From his neck, over his chest and the front of the coat, there hangs a drilled copper mirror - it is circular, and while the edge band is all copper, only the top of the edge is weathered with verdigris green; the bottom is shiny and ruddy. On the shaman's head is a fitted bear-skull helmet.
actions: Reluctantly accepts selection into warband by oracle's prophecy.
Prepares foreign mount for journey despite warriors' mockery.
Learns foreign language through patient observation around campfire.
Refutes abstract argument through demonstrative action.
Critiques rival's foreign luxury-seeking as pointless.
Positions self near danger to mock incompetent practitioner.
Searches raided dwelling and discovers enemy's cult icon.
Gets captured by antagonist in enemy stronghold
Warns pious worshipper of absurdity in their posture.
Refuses religious initiation, preferring virtue to afterlife promise.
